ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies existing representation-probing methodology (training classifiers on encoder/decoder hidden vectors) to study morphology in NMT, so the core idea is not entirely new (cf. Shi et al., 2016; Adi et al., 2016 referenced). Its novelty lies in the focused, crosslinguistic study of morphologically-rich languages, comparison of encoder vs. decoder representations (including the attention ablation), and evaluation of character vs. word-based inputs—extensions that are useful but incremental rather than fundamentally new.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important gap—what NMT models learn about morphology—by systematically evaluating encoder vs. decoder representations, word- vs. character-based inputs, depth, and target language effects across multiple morphologically-rich language pairs (Arabic, German, French, Czech, Arabic–Hebrew, Arabic–German). Its findings (e.g., encoder representations are much more informative than decoder ones as shown in Section 5 and Table 4, character CNNs yield better POS/morphology accuracy, and attention changes the encoder/decoder division of labor) are likely to influence how researchers design and analyze NMT models and motivate follow-up work on representation learning and decoder modeling. These insights have clear applicability across MT research and broader NLP work on morphology and multilingual representation learning.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper runs a fairly comprehensive set of experiments across multiple language pairs (Arabic, German, French, Czech, Arabic–Hebrew, Arabic–German), compares word vs. character CNN representations, and performs useful ablations such as encoder vs. decoder and attention vs. no-attention (Sections 3–5 and 5.1). Related work is surveyed broadly and placed appropriately (Section 6). However, some limitations remain: certain analyses (decoder-side) rely only on predicted tags due to lack of gold annotations (footnote in Section 5), and many implementation/dataset details are deferred to supplementary material/appendix (e.g., Table 1, tagger details, CNN specifics), which hampers full reproducibility and prevents assessment of statistical robustness.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper fits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it studies neural MT representation learning and interpretability, using standard probing methodology (training POS/morphology classifiers on encoder/decoder features) and evaluates across multiple language pairs including morphologically-rich languages (Arabic, German, French, Czech, Arabic–Hebrew) using WIT3/IWSLT data. It examines core modeling choices relevant to the community (word vs. char representations, encoder vs. decoder, attention ablation) and situates itself within prior work (e.g., Shi et al., Vylomova et al.), employing evaluation standards and analyses expected at these venues.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a generally logical structure (Data → Encoder/Decoder Analysis → Related Work) and states its goals early (explicit questions (i)–(iii)), and notation like ENC/DEC and ENCi(s) is introduced and used consistently. However, the provided text contains numerous presentation problems—repeated/duplicated paragraphs (especially in Related Work), formatting artifacts and broken words (e.g. 'oneby-one', 'morphol-', 'ArabicHebrew')—that substantially hinder readability. Figures/tables are referenced and informative (e.g. Table 4 POS accuracies, Figure 1), but their formatting and placement in the excerpt are cluttered, and several sentences are grammatically awkward, so some sections are hard to follow.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper performs thorough internal comparisons (word vs. char representations, encoder vs. decoder, attention vs. no-attention) on the same WIT3/IWSLT data and reports baselines (e.g. majority baseline noted for decoder results), which supports fair experimental comparisons. It also cites and positions itself relative to recent analyses (e.g. "Our work is most similar to Shi et al. (2016)" and discussion of Vylomova et al. (2016)) and reviews relevant representation methods (BPE, word-piece, character CNNs). However, it does not present direct quantitative comparisons to prior methods' published scores or evaluate alternative morphological modelling approaches (e.g. factored models or BPE/word-piece) in the same experimental setup, a minor omission.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main empirical claims (encoder representations encode morphology well; decoder representations are weaker; character-CNN improves encoder POS/morphology accuracy; attention shifts representation burden between encoder and decoder) are clearly stated and supported by cross-lingual experiments and encoder/decoder/attention ablations (e.g. Table 4 and the attention/no-attention comparison). There are no mathematical derivations to check. The experimental methodology is broadly appropriate (multiple language pairs, gold vs. predicted tags, frozen-feature classifiers, BLEU reporting) but lacks reported statistical significance, full classifier/hyperparameter details, and does not control for the confound introduced by extracting decoder features under teacher-forcing (the decoder was given gold target words), which weakens causal claims about what the decoder ‘‘learns’’ in generation. Overall the conclusions are largely justified by the presented results, but some interpretations (e.g. encoder being ‘‘language-independent’’ or the decoder merely learning a language model) are not conclusively proven and would benefit from additional controls.
